14. [REVIEW] Generalization

14.1. Adversarial Examples

  • An adversarial perturbation satisfies

    (45)

    where is some wrong class.

  • We can find one using projected gradient ascent (PGA): update and project back into the feasible set .

  • We can defend against this via stochastic gradient descent and robust optimization, e.g. adaptive data augmentation:

  • There can be some unintended features correlated with the label, such as a guy holding pictures of fish, or cats wearing bowties. These are called shortcuts.

    These can deliver stronger gradients than semantic structure. Funny example is a CNN predicting the hospital of origin better than diagnosing disease.

14.2. Distribution Shift

  • Standard supervised learning assumes that training and test data are drawn iid from the same underlying distribution.

  • We can perturb data points / the entire training distribution to obtain distributionally robust optimization (DRO):

    • Both this and adaptive data augmentation we saw above use “max” over noise so that we have guaranteed upper bounds for small distribution shifts.
  • DRO can be used to address class imbalance, paying more attention to the minority class.

  • Other ways to handle distribution shift:

    • Learn prior for subpopulation shift: e.g. [REVIEW] use Baye’s law to write

    (46)

    where is the image and is the meaningful structure like region and time, and is a prior.

    • Domain adaptation: useful for when we are moving from environment A to B (e.g. simulated to detector data) and trained on A.

      • For example, train the encoder to map both to a shared feature space by making go to zero. That way a downstream classifier cannot cheat by using domain-specific shortcuts.
